title: Banking, Finance & Economic Growth
description: Ethiopian banking institutions, financial intermediaries, economic growth vs development, and the business cycle

questions.statement: Banks act as intermediaries between depositors and borrowers.
questions.payload: TRUE. Banks channel funds from savers to those who need loans for investment.

questions.statement: The National Bank of Ethiopia takes deposits from individual members of the public.
questions.payload: FALSE. NBE is a central bank that regulates the system; it does not take public deposits.

questions.statement: The Commercial Bank of Ethiopia takes public deposits and gives loans to households and firms.
questions.payload: TRUE. CBE is a commercial bank that handles everyday deposit and lending activities.

questions.statement: The Development Bank of Ethiopia provides long-term project loans for agriculture and industry.
questions.payload: TRUE. DBE specialises in project-based lending for development sectors.

questions.statement: Modern banking in Ethiopia started in 1991.
questions.payload: FALSE. Modern banking began in 1905 with the Bank of Abyssinia under Emperor Menelik II.

questions.statement: Microfinance institutions in Ethiopia typically require collateral before giving loans.
questions.payload: FALSE. Microfinance provides small loans without collateral using group lending and peer pressure.

questions.statement: Iqub is an indigenous rotating savings group where members contribute regularly and take turns receiving the pooled money.
questions.payload: TRUE. Iqub is based on social ties with low administrative costs and no minimum threshold.

questions.statement: Iddir is primarily an investment club for generating profit among members.
questions.payload: FALSE. Iddir is a mutual assistance group mainly for funeral and emergency expenses.

questions.statement: Economic growth and economic development mean exactly the same thing.
questions.payload: FALSE. Growth is quantitative GDP increase; development is broader improvement in living standards.

questions.statement: Economic growth is necessary but not sufficient for economic development.
questions.payload: TRUE. A country can grow without developing if inequality rises and the poor see no benefit.

questions.statement: GDP per capita is a perfect measure of a country's welfare.
questions.payload: FALSE. GDP per capita ignores income distribution, home production, environmental damage, and leisure time.

questions.statement: GDP per capita counts home production such as cooking and farming for own use.
questions.payload: FALSE. GDP per capita excludes home production, informal sector activity, and volunteer work.

questions.statement: The business cycle is a regular and predictable fluctuation in economic activity.
questions.payload: FALSE. The business cycle is irregular and largely unpredictable in timing and severity.

questions.statement: During a boom, output, employment, prices, and investment are all rising.
questions.payload: TRUE. A boom is the peak phase where all major economic variables move upward.

questions.statement: During a depression, investment is at its lowest and unemployment is at its highest.
questions.payload: TRUE. Depression is the trough where confidence, output, and investment hit rock bottom.

questions.statement: During a recession, all economic variables fall immediately and simultaneously.
questions.payload: FALSE. Recession is a slowdown; variables decelerate rather than all crashing at once.

questions.statement: Agriculture employs the majority of Ethiopians and remains rain-dependent.
questions.payload: TRUE. Agriculture is the largest employer and is vulnerable to drought and climate variability.

questions.statement: Services is the fastest-growing sector in Ethiopia's economy.
questions.payload: TRUE. The services sector including trade, finance, and telecom has been the fastest-growing sector.

questions.statement: Structural transformation means shifting resources from industry to agriculture.
questions.payload: FALSE. Structural transformation shifts labour from low-productivity agriculture to industry and services.

questions.statement: Ethiopia's industrial parks policy includes locations such as Bole Lemi and Hawassa.
questions.payload: TRUE. Industrial parks are a key government strategy to promote export-led manufacturing.
